profile.name: Leslie Kean
roles: Co-Author — 2017 New York Times AATIP investigation
Author — 'UFOs: Generals, Pilots and Government Officials Go on the Record'
Founder — Coalition for Freedom of Information
profile.summary: Leslie Kean is the investigative journalist most directly responsible for triggering the modern UAP disclosure era. On December 16, 2017, she co-authored 'Glowing Auras and Black Money: The Pentagon's Mysterious UFO Program' with Helene Cooper and Ralph Blumenthal in the New York Times — the article that revealed AATIP's existence, named Luis Elizondo, published the Nimitz Tic Tac video, and launched the chain of events that led to congressional hearings, the UAP Task Force, and AARO. This outcome required a decade of groundwork: her 2010 book 'UFOs: Generals, Pilots and Government Officials Go on the Record,' built entirely on named, credentialed military and government sources from seven countries, established her credibility and demonstrated that serious, sourceable UAP reporting was possible. Her methodology throughout has been defined by insistence on named witnesses with verifiable credentials, primary documentation, and institutional accountability — a standard that distinguishes her work from most UAP journalism and made the New York Times willing to publish.

key_events.event: Obtains a copy of the French government's COMETA report — a 90-page assessment by a group of senior retired French officials and scientists concluding that some UAP were of extraterrestrial origin. The report's institutional credibility convinces her to pursue serious UAP investigation.

Founds the Coalition for Freedom of Information, a nonprofit organization using FOIA requests to obtain government UAP-related documents, with particular focus on the 1965 Kecksburg, Pennsylvania UFO incident.

Spends a decade building relationships with named, credentialed military and government sources globally — retired generals, aviation safety investigators, former intelligence officials from Belgium, France, Chile, Peru, Ecuador, the UK, and the U.S. — willing to go on record about their UAP experiences and assessments.

Publishes 'UFOs: Generals, Pilots and Government Officials Go on the Record' (Harmony Books). The book avoids anonymous sources and sensationalism; contributors include the former chiefs of staff of the Belgian and Chilean air forces, a former governor of Arizona, and retired U.S. military aviators. It becomes a New York Times bestseller and is broadly reviewed as the most credible mainstream UAP book published to date.

Continues developing sources and building relationships with the network that would eventually include Luis Elizondo, Chris Mellon, and other TTSA-affiliated figures whose disclosures she would help bring to the Times.

The New York Times publishes 'Glowing Auras and Black Money: The Pentagon's Mysterious UFO Program,' co-authored by Kean, Helene Cooper, and Ralph Blumenthal. The article reveals AATIP, names Elizondo, publishes the Nimitz Tic Tac video, and forces the U.S. government to acknowledge it had been secretly funding UAP research. The article is immediately viewed millions of times and dominates global news coverage.

Co-authors a follow-up New York Times investigation confirming that the Navy has determined the released UAP videos are authentic and that official UAP reporting guidelines are being updated. The Pentagon subsequently confirms the videos' authenticity.
The New York Times publishes additional Kean-contributed UAP reporting as congressional and military interest expands, including coverage of classified briefings and new sightings by Navy aviators

Publishes 'Surviving Death: A Journalist Investigates Evidence for an Afterlife' (Crown) — a companion investigation to her UAP work, applying the same sourcing methodology to near-death experiences, mediumship, and consciousness research

nyt_2017.title: Glowing Auras and 'Black Money': The Pentagon's Mysterious UFO Program
nyt_2017.significance: The single most consequential piece of UAP journalism ever published. It revealed AATIP's existence for the first time, named Luis Elizondo as its director, credited Senator Harry Reid with securing its $22 million funding, and published the Nimitz Tic Tac gun-camera video. The article forced the U.S. government to formally acknowledge it had operated a classified UAP research program. It triggered the cascade of events that produced the UAP Task Force (2020), the landmark congressional hearing (2023), and AARO.
key_revelations: Existence of the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) — previously classified
Luis Elizondo named as director of AATIP
Senator Harry Reid identified as political architect of AATIP's $22 million funding
Robert Bigelow's company (BAASS) named as primary AATIP contractor
Nimitz FLIR1 (Tic Tac) video published for the first time
Navy aviators quoted confirming genuine, unexplained aerial encounters

book_2010.title: UFOs: Generals, Pilots and Government Officials Go on the Record
book_2010.significance: The definitive pre-2017 mainstream UAP book. Built entirely on named, credentialed sources willing to go on record — a methodology that distinguished it from all prior mainstream UAP literature. Became a New York Times bestseller and was reviewed positively even by skeptical mainstream outlets. Contributors include retired generals, aviation safety officials, and senior government figures from seven countries. The book's existence and reception established Kean's credibility with the Times a decade before the AATIP story.
notable_contributors: General Wilfried De Brouwer (Belgian Air Force Chief of Staff) — on Belgian UAP wave (1989-1990)
General Ricardo Bermudez (Chilean CEFAA director) — on Chilean Air Force UAP investigations
Nick Pope (UK Ministry of Defence UAP desk) — on MoD investigation methodology
John Podesta (White House Chief of Staff under Clinton, Counselor to President Obama) — foreword

methodology.named_sources_only: Kean's defining editorial standard is the refusal to rely on anonymous sources for core factual claims. Every significant assertion in her UAP work is attributed to named, identifiable individuals with verifiable credentials and institutional positions.
methodology.credential_threshold: She specifically sought sources with institutional standing that made their accounts independently credible — retired generals, active pilots, government investigators, and senior officials — rather than civilian witnesses whose accounts could not be externally verified.
methodology.foia_documentation: Through the Coalition for Freedom of Information, Kean pursued documentary evidence through official government channels, treating the paper trail as essential corroboration for witness accounts.
methodology.mainstream_publication_strategy: By insisting on named sources and documented evidence, Kean made UAP reporting publishable in mainstream outlets like the New York Times that would not otherwise have engaged with the subject. This strategy ultimately proved decisive.

associated_people.name: Helene Cooper
associated_people.relationship: Co-authored the December 2017 NYT AATIP investigation. Cooper's Pentagon beat gave the story institutional credibility at the Times and access to DoD responses. Her byline alongside Kean's signaled to editors that the story met the Times' sourcing standards.

associated_people.name: Ralph Blumenthal
associated_people.relationship: Co-authored the December 2017 NYT AATIP investigation. Blumenthal is a Pulitzer Prize-winning Times veteran whose involvement reinforced the story's journalistic legitimacy.

associated_people.name: Luis Elizondo
associated_people.relationship: Elizondo was Kean's primary government source for the 2017 NYT article. His decision to cooperate with Kean's investigation, timed with his DoD resignation, was the disclosure event that made the article possible. Kean's reputation for protecting sources and demanding documentation made her the trusted recipient of his disclosure.

associated_people.name: Christopher Mellon
associated_people.role: Former DASD Intelligence; TTSA senior advisor
associated_people.relationship: Mellon facilitated the Navy videos' release to the media and was part of the source network that enabled the 2017 article. His institutional credibility as a former senior Defense Department official reinforced the story's basis.

associated_people.name: John Podesta
associated_people.role: White House Chief of Staff (Clinton); Counselor to President Obama; Clinton campaign chairman
associated_people.relationship: Wrote the foreword to Kean's 2010 book, lending it significant political credibility. Podesta's publicly stated view that the U.S. government should release UAP information was documented as early as 2002 and reinforced Kean's sourcing network.

disclosures.title: UFOs: Generals, Pilots and Government Officials Go on the Record
disclosures.notes: Landmark book establishing the named-source methodology for UAP journalism. New York Times bestseller. Set the foundation for the AATIP disclosure seven years later.

disclosures.title: Glowing Auras and 'Black Money': The Pentagon's Mysterious UFO Program
disclosures.interviewer: N/A (co-authored with Helene Cooper and Ralph Blumenthal)
disclosures.notes: The most consequential UAP article ever published. Triggered the entire modern disclosure sequence.

disclosures.title: Navy Pilots Report Unexplained Flying Objects
disclosures.notes: Follow-up investigation confirming authenticity of Navy UAP videos and reporting new encounters by F/A-18 pilots off the East Coast. Pentagon subsequently formally confirmed the videos' authenticity.

disclosures.title: Surviving Death: A Journalist Investigates Evidence for an Afterlife
disclosures.notes: Application of Kean's investigative methodology to consciousness research and afterlife evidence. Connected to her broader interest in phenomena that challenge conventional scientific paradigms.

sources.title: New York Times — 'Glowing Auras and Black Money' (2017)
sources.notes: The primary disclosure article; co-authored by Kean

sources.title: UFOs: Generals, Pilots and Government Officials Go on the Record (2010)
sources.notes: Kean's foundational book; primary source on her methodology and pre-2017 source network

sources.title: New York Times — 'Navy Pilots Report Unexplained Flying Objects' (2019)
sources.notes: Follow-up investigation corroborating and expanding the 2017 AATIP disclosures
